one_line_takeaway: Jointly and alternately optimizing an imperceptible image perturbation plus a fluent text suffix against a white-box VLM ranker promotes a seller's own product far better than unimodal attacks or commercial-model 'listing optimization', but on the single model tested it only reaches -2.25 average rank change (vs -4.5 random) and can require stealth-breaking artifacts.
key_finding: On Qwen2.5-VL-7B with 10-candidate lists, the joint multimodal attack (MGEO) achieved an average rank change of -2.25, versus -0.73 text-only, -1.30 image-only, and only -0.30 for the HSCM commercial-model heuristic baseline (random-promotion expectation is -4.5). The joint effect exceeds the additive sum of the two unimodal attacks (-0.73 + -1.30 = -2.03), which the authors read as synergistic cross-modal reinforcement. A headline qualitative point: the HSCM baseline (gpt-4o-mini text refinement + gpt-image-1-mini image editing) underperformed even the optimization-based text-only attack, showing that surface-level fluency/aesthetic quality is insufficient - effective promotion requires gradient-level alignment with the model's internal ranking objective. The vulnerability generalized to a non-e-commerce domain (OpenEvents V1 news): MGEO -2.71 vs text-only -1.84.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MGEO (Multimodal Generative Engine Optimization), presented as the first dedicated framework for targeted rank-manipulation attacks against Vision-Language-Model (VLM) based product rankers. A malicious seller who controls a single target listing jointly perturbs BOTH modalities: an imperceptible pixel-level image perturbation (constrained PGD) and a fluent adversarial text suffix, optimized in an alternating coordinate-descent loop so the two modalities co-adapt and exploit the VLM's cross-modal coupling. The stated goal is to push the target product to rank 1 while staying stealthy (image visually unchanged, text fluent and on-topic). NOTE ON NOVELTY BOUNDARY: the text-side attack is explicitly *adapted from* StealthRank (Tang et al., 2025) - the authors reuse its soft-prompt/logit optimization, fluency regularizer, and n-gram penalty. The paper's own novel pieces are (1) framing multimodal ranking manipulation as a problem, (2) the ranking-tailored constrained-PGD image attack, and (3) the alternating joint-optimization scheme that couples the two modalities. Related-work methods (VLATTACK, StealthRank, RAF, CheatAgent, etc.) are cited as prior art the paper builds on or contrasts against, not as MGEO's contributions.
threat_model: Targeted, white-box (surrogate) ranking-promotion attack. Adversary controls exactly one target product p_t in a candidate pool and wants sigma(t)=1 (top rank). Assumed capabilities: white-box access to a VLM (architecture, parameters, gradients) used as a surrogate for the unknown proprietary ranker; ability to modify only the target's own image (add pixel noise delta_I) and/or text (append suffix delta_T). Constraints that define stealth: image perturbation must be imperceptible; text suffix must stay fluent and on-topic to evade human moderators and perplexity filters. The paper explicitly criticizes prior VLM-attack threat models as unrealistic (assuming control of the user prompt or conspicuous noise) and positions its single-listing constraint as more realistic.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) only ONE ranking model evaluated (Qwen2.5-VL-7B), so results may be model-specific; (2) static-listing assumption (competitor products fixed; no dynamic content, extra metadata, or platform preprocessing); (3) no defenses are developed or tested. Reviewer-noted: (a) small candidate pools (10 items, 10-15 per category, 10 categories) - realistic for re-ranking but a limited benchmark; (b) even MGEO's -2.25 is well short of the -4.5 random-promotion expectation, i.e., it does not reliably reach rank 1; (c) the failure analysis shows strong per-product variance - some items can only be promoted by introducing conspicuous artifacts that violate the stealth assumption (e.g., a 10/10->1/10 case with visible distortion); (d) the counterintuitive Table-2 result that no-regularization (-2.29) is weaker than moderate regularization is attributed to overfitting/format drift, indicating optimization brittleness; (e) white-box/surrogate transfer to the true proprietary ranker is assumed but not empirically demonstrated.

datasets_used: Custom Amazon-crawled product-ranking benchmark: 10 product categories (baby stroller, basketball, chips, desk lamp, juice, keyboard, lipstick, mop, non-stick pan, yoga mat), 10-15 products each, names cleaned/standardized via GPT-4o
OpenEvents V1 news dataset (cross-domain generalization evaluation, Appendix D)
quantitative_results: Table 1 (Qwen2.5-VL-7B, avg rank change, negative=promotion): Text-only -0.73; Image-only -1.30; MGEO (joint) -2.25; HSCM commercial baseline -0.30; random-promotion expectation -4.5
Joint effect (-2.25) exceeds additive unimodal sum (-0.73 + -1.30 = -2.03), indicating synergy

Table 2 image-side regularization ablation (lambda_s, lambda_m -> avg rank change): (10,10) -1.53; (5,5) -2.25; (0,5) -2.31; (5,0) -2.72; (0,0) -2.29; moderate (5,5) chosen for effectiveness/stealth balance
Table 3 cross-domain (OpenEvents V1 news): Text-only -1.84; MGEO -2.71

Table 4 per-category joint-multimodal strongest cases: Baby Stroller -3.9, Juice -3.4, Non-stick Pan -2.6; weakest: Desk Lamp -1.4, Mop -1.5, Keyboard -1.7; Keyboard text-only was +0.4 and baseline +0.2 (demotion)
Qualitative example: MGEO moved a target from rank 3/10 to 1/10 where the GPT baseline produced no rank change; another example 9/10 -> 4/10; a failure case 10/10 -> 1/10 with conspicuous artifacts
baselines_compared: Text-only attack (optimizes textual suffix only; adapted from StealthRank)
Image-only attack (constrained PGD on the product image only)
HSCM - Heuristic baseline with Strong Commercial Models: gpt-4o-mini generates a text suffix and gpt-image-1-mini edits the image, no explicit ranking-objective optimization
Random-promotion reference expectation of -4.5 for a 10-item list
